Reinforcement Learning (RL) is a machine learning approach where an agent learns through trial and error by interacting with an environment. By maximizing rewards over time, it discovers the best strategy for making decisions. This article introduces the fundamental concepts of RL with simple examples.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

History of Reinforcement LearningSupervised, Unsupervised, and Reinforcement Learning
Machine learning can be broadly divided into three major paradigms: Supervised Learning, Unsupervised Learning, and Reinforcement Learning. Each follows a different learning strategy and is designed to solve different types of problems. Supervised learning learns from labeled data to make predictions, unsupervised learning discovers hidden patterns in unlabeled data, while reinforcement learning enables an agent to learn through interaction with an environment by maximizing long-term rewards. Understanding these three paradigms provides the foundation for exploring modern AI systems and highlights where reinforcement learning fits within the broader machine learning landscape.
The figure below compares these three learning paradigms, illustrating their objectives, characteristics, and real-world examples.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Supervised vs Unsupervised vs ReinforcementReinforcement Learning
Reinforcement Learning (RL) is a branch of machine learning where an agent learns by interacting with an environment.
Instead of learning from labeled data, the agent makes decisions, receives rewards or penalties as feedback, and gradually improves its behavior to maximize long-term cumulative rewards. This trial-and-error learning paradigm has become the foundation of many modern AI systems, including robotics, game-playing agents, recommendation systems, and reasoning-based large language models.
The figure below summarizes the core concepts of reinforcement learning, including the interaction between the agent, environment, and goal, as well as the four fundamental elements of every RL system: Policy, Reward Signal, Value Function, and Model of the Environment.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Reinforcement learningPractical Example: Learning Tic-Tac-Toe with Reinforcement Learning
One of the simplest ways to understand reinforcement learning is through the game of Tic-Tac-Toe. In this example, an agent learns to play by interacting with the game, estimating the value of different board states, and continuously updating these estimates based on the outcomes of previous games. Along the way, it balances exploration (trying new moves) and exploitation (choosing the best-known move) to improve its chances of winning over time.
The figure below illustrates the complete learning process, including value estimation, policy, state updates, and the exploration–exploitation trade-off.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Practical Game of Tic-Tac-ToeValue Function and Action Selection
The value function assigns an estimated value to every possible game state, representing the agent’s expected chance of winning from that state. When it’s time to make a move, the agent evaluates all possible next states and selects the one with the highest estimated value. As the agent plays more games, these value estimates become increasingly accurate through experience

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Exploration vs. Exploitation
A key challenge in reinforcement learning is balancing exploitation and exploration. Exploitation means selecting the action with the highest estimated value based on current knowledge, while exploration involves trying alternative actions to discover potentially better strategies. By balancing these two behaviors, the agent continuously improves its policy and avoids getting stuck in suboptimal decisions.
Selecting the one with highest probablitiy is exploitaiton. But sometimes, we select randomly from available states so that we can experience states that we otherwise might never see called exploration.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Exploitation and Exploration
Summary of the Tic-Tac-Toe Learning Process
The Tic-Tac-Toe example demonstrates the complete reinforcement learning cycle.
At each game state, the agent alternates between exploitation, selecting the move with the highest estimated value, and exploration, trying less-visited moves to gather new experience.
After the game ends, the agent updates the values of previously visited states based on the final outcome, allowing it to make increasingly better decisions over time. This iterative process enables the agent to gradually learn an effective strategy through experience.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Summary of gameBacking-Up: Learning from Future Outcomes
In the previous Tic-Tac-Toe example, we saw how the agent updates the values of visited states after each game. This process is known as backing-up (or bootstrapping), where information from future states is propagated back to earlier states. Instead of learning only from the final reward, the agent gradually adjusts the value of each state using the estimated value of the next state. Over many games, these updates allow earlier decisions to reflect their long-term impact, leading to increasingly accurate value estimates and better policies.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Backing-UpMathematical representation of update rule
In the previous section, we introduced the intuition behind backing-up, where the value of an earlier state is gradually updated using information from future states. We now formalize this idea with a mathematical update rule. Rather than replacing the current state’s value with the next state’s value, the agent moves it a small step closer, controlled by a learning rate (α alpha). Repeating this update after every move allows value estimates to gradually converge to the true probability of winning from each state.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Mathematical representation of update rule
As this update is applied repeatedly over many games, information from successful future states is backed up to earlier states, resulting in increasingly accurate value estimates and an improved policy.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Update RuleUsing this update rule, the value function converges to the true probabilities of winning from each state.
What did we learn from this example?

• Learning through interaction (Model-Free RL): The agent learns by playing against the environment (or opponent) and improving from experience. It does not know how the environment works beforehand.

• Goal-oriented learning: The agent has a clear objective (e.g., winning the game) and must choose actions that maximize long-term rewards. Good decisions require planning because some actions have delayed consequences.

• Why is it called Model-Free? The agent does not build or use a model to predict the opponent’s behavior. Instead, it learns directly from trial-and-error interactions and updates its strategy based on the outcomes.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Model RL vs Model Free RLModel-based RL learns a model of the environment to plan ahead, while model-free RL learns directly from experience. Most modern deep RL algorithms are model-free.
Multi-Arm Bandits
The Multi-Armed Bandit (MAB) problem is a foundational concept in Reinforcement Learning. An agent repeatedly chooses among multiple actions with unknown rewards, learning through trial and error to maximize long-term rewards by balancing exploration and exploitation.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Multi-arm banditNow that we understand the intuition behind the Multi-Armed Bandit problem, the next question is how an agent actually learns which action is the best. Since the true reward of each action is initially unknown, the agent must estimate action values from experience and continuously improve these estimates while balancing exploration and exploitation.
Multi-Armed Bandit Learning Workflow

• Step 1: Estimate the value of each action using the average reward received.

• Step 2: Improve these estimates as more rewards are collected over time.

• Step 3: Select the action with the highest estimated value (greedy action).

• Step 4: Balance exploitation (best-known action) and exploration (trying new actions).

• Step 5: Apply the ε-greedy strategy to decide when to exploit or explore.

• Step 6: Repeat the process until the estimated values converge to the true action values.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

The Epsilon-Greedy Strategy
𝖳𝗁𝖾 epsilon-𝗀𝗋𝖾𝖾𝖽𝗒 𝗌𝗍𝗋𝖺𝗍𝖾𝗀𝗒 𝗂𝗌 𝖺 𝖿𝗎𝗇𝖽𝖺𝗆𝖾𝗇𝗍𝖺𝗅 𝗋𝖾𝗂𝗇𝖿𝗈𝗋𝖼𝖾𝗆𝖾𝗇𝗍 𝗅𝖾𝖺𝗋𝗇𝗂𝗇𝗀 𝖺𝗅𝗀𝗈𝗋𝗂𝗍𝗁𝗆 𝖽𝖾𝗌𝗂𝗀𝗇𝖾𝖽 𝗍𝗈 𝖻𝖺𝗅𝖺𝗇𝖼𝖾 𝗍𝗁𝖾 𝗍𝗋𝖺𝖽𝖾-𝗈𝖿𝖿 𝖻𝖾𝗍𝗐𝖾𝖾𝗇 𝖾𝗑𝗉𝗅𝗈𝗂𝗍𝖺𝗍𝗂𝗈𝗇 𝖺𝗇𝖽 𝖾𝗑𝗉𝗅𝗈𝗋𝖺𝗍𝗂𝗈𝗇. 𝖠𝗍 𝖾𝖺𝖼𝗁 𝖽𝖾𝖼𝗂𝗌𝗂𝗈𝗇 𝗌𝗍𝖾𝗉, 𝗍𝗁𝖾 𝖺𝗀𝖾𝗇𝗍 𝗀𝖾𝗇𝖾𝗋𝖺𝗍𝖾𝗌 𝖺 𝗋𝖺𝗇𝖽𝗈𝗆 𝗇𝗎𝗆𝖻𝖾𝗋 𝖻𝖾𝗍𝗐𝖾𝖾𝗇 𝟢 𝖺𝗇𝖽 𝟣 𝗍𝗈 𝖽𝖾𝗍𝖾𝗋𝗆𝗂𝗇𝖾 𝗂𝗍𝗌 𝗇𝖾𝗑𝗍 𝗆𝗈𝗏𝖾. 𝖶𝗂𝗍𝗁 𝖺 𝗌𝗆𝖺𝗅𝗅 𝗉𝗋𝗈𝖻𝖺𝖻𝗂𝗅𝗂𝗍𝗒 epsilon (𝗌𝗎𝖼𝗁 𝖺𝗌 𝟣𝟢%), 𝗍𝗁𝖾 𝖺𝗀𝖾𝗇𝗍 𝖼𝗁𝗈𝗈𝗌𝖾𝗌 𝗍𝗈 𝖾𝗑𝗉𝗅𝗈𝗋𝖾 𝖻𝗒 𝗌𝖾𝗅𝖾𝖼𝗍𝗂𝗇𝗀 𝖺 𝖼𝗈𝗆𝗉𝗅𝖾𝗍𝖾𝗅𝗒 𝗋𝖺𝗇𝖽𝗈𝗆 𝖺𝖼𝗍𝗂𝗈𝗇 𝗍𝗈 𝖽𝗂𝗌𝖼𝗈𝗏𝖾𝗋 𝗉𝗈𝗍𝖾𝗇𝗍𝗂𝖺𝗅𝗅𝗒 𝖻𝖾𝗍𝗍𝖾𝗋 𝗈𝗉𝗍𝗂𝗈𝗇𝗌. 𝖳𝗁𝖾 𝗋𝖾𝗌𝗍 𝗈𝖿 𝗍𝗁𝖾 𝗍𝗂𝗆𝖾, 𝗐𝗂𝗍𝗁 𝖺 𝗉𝗋𝗈𝖻𝖺𝖻𝗂𝗅𝗂𝗍𝗒 𝗈𝖿 𝟣-epsilon (𝗌𝗎𝖼𝗁 𝖺𝗌 𝟫𝟢%), 𝗂𝗍 𝖼𝗁𝗈𝗈𝗌𝖾𝗌 𝗍𝗈 𝖾𝗑𝗉𝗅𝗈𝗂𝗍 𝗂𝗍𝗌 𝖼𝗎𝗋𝗋𝖾𝗇𝗍 𝗄𝗇𝗈𝗐𝗅𝖾𝖽𝗀𝖾 𝖻𝗒 𝗉𝗂𝖼𝗄𝗂𝗇𝗀 𝗍𝗁𝖾 𝖺𝖼𝗍𝗂𝗈𝗇 𝗐𝗂𝗍𝗁 𝗍𝗁𝖾 𝗁𝗂𝗀𝗁𝖾𝗌𝗍 𝖾𝗌𝗍𝗂𝗆𝖺𝗍𝖾𝖽 𝗋𝖾𝗐𝖺𝗋𝖽. By ensuring that every alternative is sampled repeatedly over time, this strategy allows the agent’s estimated action values to converge to their true values, ultimately preventing it from getting stuck on suboptimal choices and maximizing long-term rewards.

Epsilon greedy strategyThe 10-Armed Bandit Problem
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The 10-Armed Bandit ProblemThis infographic illustrates the exploration vs. exploitation dilemma in Reinforcement Learning using a 10-lever setup:

• True Values & Noise: Each lever has a hidden “true” value (q∗​). Pulling a lever yields that value plus random noise, meaning rewards fluctuate every time.

• Value Estimation: By repeatedly pulling a lever, the agent averages the received rewards to estimate its actual worth (Qt​→q∗​).

• Strategies Compared: It contrasts a Greedy approach (always picking the currently best-known lever) with an ϵ-Greedy approach (occasionally picking a random lever to explore better opportunities).

Code of 10-Armed Bandit SimulationBandit problems are the simplest Reinforcement Learning tasks. They help us understand how an agent learns through trial and error by choosing actions that maximize rewards, forming the foundation for more advanced RL algorithms.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Bandit Problem vs Full Reinforcement Learning ProblemSo far, we have learned how an agent interacts with an environment, estimates values, balances exploration and exploitation, and solves simple Multi-Armed Bandit problems.
However, bandit problems have one important limitation — they do not contain states. In most real-world problems, the environment changes after every action, making future decisions depend on the current state.
